Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

I don't spend hours there, just a little bit when I have time, maybe to see what my friends are up to or to look at something nice, like a picture from a trip. But even in those short moments, you see things that just don't feel right, stories that are too dramatic or pictures that look too perfect, and you start to wonder if it’s all true. For me, dealing with this fake stuff isn't about becoming a detective, because that is too much trouble for someone like me who is busy with my work and my family. It’s more about slowing down and trusting my own good sense, the way my mother always taught me to judge things. If something sounds too shocking, or if it makes my heart beat too fast—that feeling of worry or anger—then I stop. I don't immediately share it. Instead, I take a breath, just like when I'm taking a break from scrubbing floors, and I ask myself: does this come from someone I truly know, or is it some stranger trying to make a fuss? If it is something important, like news about our community, I look for a second, reliable source, maybe something from the local paper or someone I trust completely. If it’s just some silly drama, I let it pass by. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When I look at these social media things, sometimes it feels like a big, noisy place, doesn't it? I don't spend hours there, just a little bit when I have time, maybe to see what my friends are up to or to look at something nice, like a picture from a trip. But even in those short moments, you see things that just don't feel right, stories that are too dramatic or pictures that look too perfect, and you start to wonder if it’s all true. For me, dealing with this fake stuff isn't about becoming a detective, because that is too much trouble for someone like me who is busy with my work and my family. It’s more about slowing down and trusting my own good sense, the way my mother always taught me to judge things. If something sounds too shocking, or if it makes my heart beat too fast—that feeling of worry or anger—then I stop. I don't immediately share it. Instead, I take a breath, just like when I'm taking a break from scrubbing floors, and I ask myself: does this come from someone I truly know, or is it some stranger trying to make a fuss? Because I value kindness and truth, which is what Islam teaches, and I don't want to spread rumors or things that cause unnecessary sadness. If it is something important, like news about our community, I look for a second, reliable source, maybe something from the local paper or someone I trust completely. If it’s just some silly drama, I let it pass by. My focus is on the real things in life—the taste of good food after baking, the feeling after a good run, or being there for my family—and I don't want these little digital lies to distract me from what is real and good.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a highly agreeable, extraverted, and somewhat anxious woman (high neuroticism) who has lower education and limited social media use. Her perspective must be grounded, practical, emotionally sensitive, and aligned with her cultural/religious values (Islam, strong sense of community/morality). The advice must be simple, actionable, and focused on emotional regulation rather than technical analysis, fitting her lower technical comfort level.
response_parsed.tone: Warm, cautious, highly agreeable, and grounded in simple, moral intuition.
